text:
Admission Date:  1986-09-16              Discharge Date:   2002-12-07

Date of Birth:  1971-11-30             Sex:   M

Service: MEDICINE

Allergies:
Penicillamine / Ciprofloxacin / Vancomycin / Insulins / Lithium

Attending:Dylan
Chief Complaint:
Transfer from surgery for further management

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
None

History of Present Illness:
66-year-old male w/MMP as below and recent admission for
aspiration PNA and flu (DCed on vanc/zosyn w/course planned to
end 07-22) admitted to the surgical ICU 1986-09-16 w/hematemesis and
bloody output from his gtube. At the time of admission, he was
s/p 2U PRBC @ Saint Joseph's Regional Medical Center - Plymouth Rehab and had experienced 200cc of bloody
emesis there. In the ED, he had dark red blood from Gtube which
did not clear s/p 400cc of lavage. His HR was 110, SBP150 at
that time. He had diffuse abdominal pain which has since been
learned to be unchanged over the past year.
.
CT abdomen and pelvis in the ED revealed partial vs early SBO
but no perforation or free air. His SICU course has been notable
for stable hematocrits, stable BP, no further evidence of
bleeding, and continued abdominal pain requiring 25-50 of
fentanyl Q2H. He was given imipenim for ?PNA. Of note, on
admission his WBC was 21.4 and crit 37.2 which have fallen c/w
time course of IVF administration.
.
Last BM was small, last PM. It was not guaiced.


Past Medical History:
Wilson's disease
Bipolar disease, hx SI
Bronchiectasis
Atrial fibrillation
s/p MVR
CAD
Pancreatitis
s/p Billroth II for peptic ulcer disease
CRI baseline cr 1.9-2.4
PSH
s/p exploratory laparotomy
s/p bowel resection for obstruction
s/p partial colectomy. He is also status post gastrostomytube
placement. S/P R hip fracture and THR. Cataracts. Anemia. Had
flu shot this year,pneumovax 1994-10-10. **MRSA/VRE**


Social History:
Lives at Suburban Community Hospital rehab, no etoh use, no tobacco


Family History:
Non-contributory.

Physical Exam:
98.3 ST103 115/26 (98-115/26-51) 20 96%2L; CVP 8
Gen: cachectic, shaking, not in distress
HENNT: MMM, anicteric, PERRL, EOMI
Neck: Supple, no JVD
CV: Tachy
Lungs: diffusely ronchorous
Abd: soft, distended, mild LUQ tenderness, hypoactive BS
Ext: no LE edema, strong DP/PT pulses bilaterally
Neuro: A&Ox3, CNII-XII intact, UE/LE muscle strength 5/5 b/l
Skin: no rash
Rectal: No palpable masses. Guaic negative. Prostate large and
non-nodular.


Pertinent Results:

2011-07-03 06:45PM BLOOD Hct-26.4*
1971-02-24 06:01AM BLOOD WBC-8.6 RBC-3.07* Hgb-8.7* Hct-26.2*
MCV-85 MCH-28.3 MCHC-33.2 RDW-17.5* Plt Ct-308.
.
CT abd/pelvis 1986-09-16: CT OF THE ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITHOUT IV
CONTRAST: A large hiatal hernia is noted. Compared to prior
study, there has been interval resolution of the previously seen
left-sided pneumothorax. There are linear and patchy opacities
seen at the lung bases bilaterally, suggesting scar as well as
possible focal areas of consolidation which could represent
infection. No effusions seen at the visualized lung bases.

The liver, spleen, pancreas, and adrenal glands appear
unremarkable allowing for limitations of a non-contrast study.
Rounded hypodensities are seen within the kidneys, likely
representing simple cysts, not fully characterized. Patient is
status post cholecystectomy. There are multiple dilated loops of
small bowel with air-fluid levels. No colon is identified. There
is a short area of bowel narrowing close to the low anastomoses.
There does appear to be fluid and gas in the rectum distal to
this area of narrowing. This appearance is not significantly
changed from prior study. The loops of small bowel may be
slightly more dilated when compared to previous study. There is
no evidence of free air or free fluid within the abdomen or
pelvis.

BONE WINDOWS: Again noted is a right hip prosthesis. Bones
appear osteopenic. Injection granulomas again noted. No
suspicious lytic or blastic lesions are identified.

Multiplanar reformatted images confirm the axial findings.

IMPRESSION:
1. Partial versus early small-bowel obstruction with area of
narrowing seen at the distal bowel in the area of the distal
anastomosis, not significantly changed from prior study.
2. Linear opacities and patchy opacities in the lung bases
bilaterally, consistent with scarring and possibly infection.
3. Otherwise, no significant change from prior study.
.
CXR 1986-09-16: Again noted is a right-sided central line with tip
overlying the SVC. Cardiomediastinal and hilar contours appear
unchanged. Pulmonary vascularity appears unremarkable. There has
been interval improvement in bilateral lower lobe
consolidations. There is increased airspace opacity again seen
in the right lung greater than left consistent with infection,
possibly blood. Poorly defined linear lucency is seen in the
medial aspect of the left hemidiaphragm.

IMPRESSION:
1. Increased airspace opacity in the right lung greater than
left, possibly representing infectious process versus blood.
2. Poorly seen linear lucency at the medial aspect of the left
hemidiaphragm. Further evaluation with dedicated PA and lateral
chest radiographs centered on this area would be helpful.
.
CXR 2011-07-03: Chest is markedly rotated to the left. Right jugular
CV line is in distal SVC. No pneumothorax. There has been no
significant change in the ill-defined patchy opacities at the
left base, similar less marked opacities at the right lung base
with some possible increased interstitial densities in the right
lung, difficult to evaluate on this rotated film. No
pneumothorax.
.
Time Taken Not Noted     Log-In Date/Time: 1986-09-16 5:56 pm
      URINE     Site: CATHETER

                            **FINAL REPORT 1989-08-01**

   URINE CULTURE (Final 1989-08-01):    <10,000 organisms/ml.
.
1989-08-01 9:00 pm STOOL     CONSISTENCY: SOFT      Source: Stool.


                            **FINAL REPORT 2011-07-03**

   CLOSTRIDIUM DIFFICILE TOXIN ASSAY (Final 2011-07-03):
      FECES NEGATIVE FOR C. DIFFICILE TOXIN BY EIA.
      Reference Range:  Negative.
.
1976-09-07 EGD: Findings: Esophagus:
Lumen: A medium size hiatal hernia was seen, displacing the
Z-line to 35cm from the incisors, with hiatal narrowing at 40cm
from the incisors. He had a wide open esophageal sphincter.
Contents: Bile was found in the stomach and small bowel.
Stomach:
Mucosa: Diffuse continuous erythema and congestion of the mucosa
were noted in the stomach body. These findings are compatible
with gastritis. There was a point of active oozing of blood
within the hiatal hernia,w hich was treated sucessfully with
BICAP cauterization. There was no discernible ulcer.
Duodenum: Normal duodenum.
Other findings: Area of the gastro-jejunal anastomosis appeared
normal.
Impression: Gastritis with point of bleeding in stomach within
hiatal hernia. This was likely source of bleed and was treated
with BICAP cautery.
Recommendations: Protonix 40 mg Twice daily
Elevate head of bed, aspiration precautions.
.
Abdominal U/S 1976-09-07: RIGHT UPPER QUADRANT ULTRASOUND: The
patient is status post cholecystectomy. The common bile duct is
not dilated. There is no intrahepatic biliary dilatation. There
are no focal liver lesions. The portal vein is patent.

IMPRESSION:
1. No evidence of intrahepatic biliary dilatation.


Brief Hospital Course:
A/P: 66 yo male with complicated medical hx, recent aspiration
pna re-admitted with GIB and ileus.
.
GIB- No patient did not require any blood transfusion here as pt
received 2 units at the Saint Joseph's Regional Medical Center - Plymouth Rehab.  Pt remained
hemodynamically stable, hct stabilized, and hematemasis and
bleeding from PEG stopped.  IV PPI Meadowbrook Rehabilitation Hospital was switched to PO PPI
Meadowbrook Rehabilitation Hospital when PEG feeding was restarted.  Pt underwent EGD on 1976-09-07
which showed gastritis with point of bleeding in stomach within
hiatal hernia which was likely source of bleed and was treated
with BICAP cautery.  The patient was restarted on tube feeding
after EGD and tolerated well.
.
Ileus/PSBO-  CT of abdomen was concerning for PSBO vs early SBO,
but these findings were not new and not significantly changed
from CT abd/pelvis in 06-03.  The patient was placed on bowel
rest initially.  As abdominal pain improved, restarted tube
feeding per surgery recs and held for EGD then was restarted
tube feeding.  Tried to avoid narcotics, have the patient out of
bed, and bowel regimens were given.
.
Normal WBC with Bandemia: The patient finished 14day course of
vanc and imipenem on 2011-07-03 for aspiration pneumonia.  Pt
initially had bandemia, and it resolved.  Blood and urine have
not had growth.  Sputum sample was obtained but was a poor
sample. The patient did not spike any fever or leukocytosis.
However, the patient will need aggressive pulmonary toilet
including good oral suctioning care and chest PT as pt is at
risk for aspiration.
.
Elevated LFTs/amylase and lipase: Pt is s/p cholecystectomy, and
pt had an MRCP in 04-10 for elevated amylase and lipase without
any remarkable abnormalities.  Abdominal u/s during this
admission did not reveal any intrahepatic dilitations or biliary
obstructions either.  As the patient had no clinical symptoms
and h/o Wilson's, recommended following clinically and LFTs.
.
CAD: Continued BB, held ASA for UGIB.
.
Bipolar DO/psych- Continued lamotragine, bupropion, klonipin,
and olanzapine
.
Wilson's dz- Placed on zinc sulfate as Nye Regional Medical Center does not carry
trientine.
.
CRI: Was stable. Renally dosed all meds.
.
FEN: Was NPO while concerned for obstruction and on TPN for a
day, then restarted TFs as tolerated. Repleted 'lytes/prn.
.
# IV access: pt had a CVL (RIJ) from previous admissioni for iv
antibiotics.  The line was removed at the time of discharge.
.
#Prophylaxis: PPI Meadowbrook Rehabilitation Hospital. Pneumoboots. Keep HOB elevated.
Aspiration precaution.


Medications on Admission:
tylenol, ibuprofin, Esomeprazole, Fludrocortisone 0.1mg daily,
guiafenesin/codine, lamotrigine 25mg Meadowbrook Rehabilitation Hospital, levoflox 500mg daily
started 4/4/6, metoprolol 50mg tid, midodrine 5mg tid,
olanzapine 10mg daily, oseltamvir 75mg daily started 4/6/6,
sucralfate, trientine 250mg Meadowbrook Rehabilitation Hospital, coombivent, trimethobenzamide
prn, hydrocortisone rectally prn.

Discharge Medications:

a day).

(Daily).

(3 times a day).

11. Acetaminophen 325 mg Tablet Sig: 1-2 Tablets PO Q4-6H (every
4 to 6 hours) as needed.
12. Ipratropium Bromide 0.02 % Solution Sig: One (1)  Inhalation
QID (4 times a day).
13. Albuterol Sulfate 0.083 % Solution Sig: One (1)  Inhalation
Q2H (every 2 hours) as needed.
14. Zinc Sulfate 220 mg Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule PO once a
day.


Discharge Disposition:
Extended Care

Facility:
Memorial Hermann Tomball Hospital for the Aged - Acute Rehab

Discharge Diagnosis:
Blood loss anemia
Gastritis


Discharge Condition:
Stable hct at 28.9, no more hematemasis or bleeding from PEG


Discharge Instructions:
Please return to the emergency department if you have fevers,
increased shortness of breath, bloody stools, black tarry
stools, vomiting blood, chest pain, or any other worrisome
symptoms.
.
Keep your follow-up appointments.  Take your medications as
instructed.

Followup Instructions:
Follow-up with Dr. Hill in a week. 705-743-1638.
